title: Cockcrow
titleNote: The title shown here follows the source page's parenthetical English rendering; the romanized source form is preserved for search and future cleanup.
description: Cockcrow is a short story collection by Ebrahim Golestan. The source page lists the work as banned in Iran.
descriptionParagraphs: Cockcrow is a short story collection by Ebrahim Golestan. The source page lists the work as banned in Iran.

The surviving record is interesting because it shows how even ordinary-looking books can acquire a charged political afterlife. As a short story collection, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in Iran. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: The source page lists the work as banned in Iran. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

banningParagraphs: Cockcrow entered censorship debates as a short story collection associated with controversy, publication history, and state scrutiny. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political sensitivity.

The earliest event currently captured here is Post-1979 Islamic Republic period in Iran, where Iranian censors and the Ministry of Culture and Islamic Guidance banned or denied publication. The source page lists the work as banned in Iran. The source page lists the work as banned in Iran.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
